title: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets
subtitle: Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A complete active-learning guide covering every major topic from your lecture slides — enriched with context, examples, and exam-ready exercises.

title: Why Financial Markets Matter
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Financial markets are the circulatory system of a modern economy. Just as blood carries oxygen and nutrients to every cell in your body, financial markets carry capital — money and investment — to every corner of economic activity. Without functioning financial markets, businesses couldn't grow, governments couldn't finance infrastructure, and individuals couldn't save for retirement or buy homes. At their core, financial markets are structures through which funds flow from those who have surplus capital (savers and investors) to those who need it (corporations, governments, individuals). This transfer of capital is the foundational service that makes economic growth possible. When you understand financial markets, you understand how your world is funded. Why this course matters
          Understanding financial markets isn't just academic — it's directly relevant to career decisions, personal investing, and understanding the news. The 2008 financial crisis, the rise of Bitcoin, and the impact of Federal Reserve interest rate decisions all trace back to concepts you'll learn in this chapter. Investment and financing decisions — whether you're a corporate manager deciding how to raise capital for a new factory, or an individual deciding where to put your savings — require a deep understanding of how funds flow through the economy. This is why financial markets are the starting point for any serious study of finance.

title: Primary vs. Secondary Markets
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The most fundamental way to classify financial markets is by whether they deal in new securities or existing ones. This distinction gives us the primary/secondary market framework.

title: Primary Markets: Where New Securities Are Born
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Primary markets are markets in which users of funds — companies, governments, municipalities — raise money by issuing brand-new financial instruments such as stocks and bonds. The defining characteristic is that the money raised goes directly to the issuer. When a corporation sells stock in a primary market, it receives the proceeds and uses them to fund operations, expand capacity, or pay down debt. The most well-known type of primary market activity is the Initial Public Offering (IPO), where a private company sells shares to the public for the first time. When Amazon went public in 1997, or when Airbnb IPO'd in 2020, these were primary market events — the companies raised fresh capital from investors who purchased newly created shares. Background: How an IPO actually works
          In a typical IPO, a company hires an investment bank (called an underwriter) to help price and sell the new shares. The underwriter buys the shares from the company and resells them to institutional investors and the public. The company gets the money; the underwriter earns a fee. This is all primary market activity. After the IPO, trading on the stock exchange (e.g., NYSE or NASDAQ) is secondary market activity.

title: Secondary Markets: Where Securities Change Hands
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Secondary markets trade financial instruments after they have already been issued. The New York Stock Exchange, NASDAQ, and bond markets are all secondary markets. When you buy Apple stock on your brokerage app, you're buying from another investor — not from Apple itself. Apple receives nothing from that transaction; you and the other investor are simply exchanging ownership of an already-existing share. This might make secondary markets sound less important, but they are actually essential to the entire financial system for three critical reasons: Liquidity is the ability to convert an asset into cash quickly at its fair market value. Secondary markets provide this. If you buy corporate bonds but later need cash, you can sell them in the secondary bond market. Without secondary markets, buying a stock or bond would be like buying a house — you'd be locked in until a private buyer came along. The existence of liquid secondary markets is precisely what makes people willing to buy securities in the primary market in the first place. Price information is the second function. Secondary market prices continuously reflect all available information about the value of investments. When Apple's stock price rises, it signals that investors collectively believe the company is worth more. These prices guide capital allocation across the entire economy. Low transaction costs are the third function. Modern secondary markets — especially electronic exchanges — enable trading at very low cost, which increases efficiency and makes markets accessible to ordinary investors. Common misconception
          Many students confuse where money goes in each market type. In a primary market, the issuer gets the money. In a secondary market, the selling investor gets the money. Buying stock on the NYSE does NOT give money to the company — it gives money to whoever sold you the share. After this initial issue, investors trade with each other in the secondary market — the issuer is no longer involved.

title: Impact of the Financial Crisis on Primary Markets
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The 2008-2009 financial crisis severely disrupted primary markets. As banks suffered catastrophic losses from mortgage-related securities, they became unwilling to underwrite new stock and bond offerings. Companies suddenly found it extremely difficult — or impossible — to raise new capital. This credit freeze caused massive economic contraction, because businesses couldn't fund operations or expansion. It illustrates a critical point: when primary markets seize up, the real economy suffers immediately and severely.

title: Money Markets vs. Capital Markets
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The second major classification divides markets by the maturity — the time until repayment — of the financial instruments traded. Trade debt instruments with maturities of one year or less. These are short-term borrowing and lending arrangements between large institutions, governments, and corporations. Most U.S. money markets are over-the-counter (OTC), meaning trades happen directly between parties, not on a centralized exchange. Trade debt (bonds) and equity (stocks) with maturities of more than one year — including stocks, which technically have no maturity date. Capital market instruments experience wider price fluctuations than money market instruments because longer time horizons create more uncertainty. Background: What are OTC markets?
          Over-the-counter (OTC) markets have no physical trading floor or centralized exchange. Instead, dealers electronically quote prices and trade directly with each other and with clients. This contrasts with exchange-traded markets like the NYSE, where all trades happen through a centralized system with publicly posted prices. Most money market instruments are too large and institutional for a retail exchange.

title: Key Capital Market Instruments
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Capital markets fund long-term investment — the kind that builds factories, infrastructure, and companies. They include: Common misconception: Why do capital markets have wider price swings?
          Students often think stocks are volatile because companies are risky, full stop. That's partly true, but the deeper reason is time. With a 30-year bond or no-maturity stock, there's enormous uncertainty about the future — interest rates, economic conditions, company performance — over that long horizon. Compare this to a 3-month T-bill: you know almost exactly what it'll be worth in 90 days. Longer time horizon = more uncertainty = wider price fluctuations.

title: Foreign Exchange Markets
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Foreign exchange (FX or forex) markets are where currencies are traded. They are the largest and most liquid financial markets in the world, with over $7 trillion traded daily. For investors and corporations operating internationally, foreign exchange markets are inescapable. The core concept your course introduces is foreign exchange risk, defined as the sensitivity of the value of cash flows on foreign investments to changes in the foreign currency's price in terms of dollars. Let's unpack that definition with an example: Imagine a U.S. company has a subsidiary in Japan that earns ¥100 million in profit. At an exchange rate of ¥150/dollar, that converts to about $667,000. But if the yen weakens to ¥200/dollar, the same ¥100 million profit converts to only $500,000 — a $167,000 loss just from currency movement, with no change in business performance. That's foreign exchange risk. This risk is two-sided: the yen could also strengthen (say, to ¥100/dollar), making those profits worth $1,000,000 in dollar terms — an FX gain. Whether this is good or bad depends on your business model and how you've managed your currency exposure. Background: How are exchange rates set?
          In modern floating-rate systems (used by most major economies), exchange rates are determined by supply and demand in FX markets. Factors that move rates include: interest rate differentials between countries, inflation rates, political stability, trade balances, and market speculation. The Federal Reserve's decisions about U.S. interest rates have a significant impact on the dollar's value relative to other currencies. Connection to derivatives
          Companies routinely use derivative instruments (specifically currency futures and options) to hedge their foreign exchange risk — locking in a future exchange rate so they aren't surprised by currency movements. This connects the FX market directly to the derivative markets you'll study next.

title: Derivative Security Markets
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A derivative security is a financial instrument whose value is derived from — and linked to — an underlying asset or security. The underlying can be a stock, bond, currency, commodity, interest rate, or almost anything else with a price. Derivatives don't stand alone; their payoff depends entirely on what happens to the underlying. Derivative markets generally involve an agreement between two parties to exchange a standard quantity of an asset or cash flow at a predetermined price at a specified future date. This simple description covers an enormous variety of instruments. An obligation to buy or sell an asset at a fixed price on a specific future date. Both parties must complete the transaction. Traded on exchanges with standardized contracts. Common in commodities (oil, wheat) and financial instruments (Treasury futures). The right but not the obligation to buy (call) or sell (put) an asset at a fixed price before or on a specific date. The buyer pays a premium for this right. Options are fundamental to corporate hedging and speculative trading strategies. Agreements to exchange cash flows over time. An interest rate swap, for example, lets one party swap a fixed interest rate for a floating rate. Swaps are massive in size: the global interest rate swap market is valued in the hundreds of trillions of dollars. Bundles of mortgage loans packaged and sold as securities. Their payoff depends on whether homeowners make their mortgage payments. MBS are why the housing market collapse in 2007–2008 triggered a global financial crisis — banks worldwide held these instruments.

title: Growth and the Volcker Rule
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Derivative markets saw tremendous growth between 1992 and 2013 — fueled by deregulation, computing power, and financial innovation. However, a large drop in derivative activity occurred from 2013 to 2019, largely due to the 2014 implementation of the Volcker Rule. Background: What is the Volcker Rule?
          The Volcker Rule, named after former Federal Reserve Chairman Paul Volcker, is part of the 2010 Dodd-Frank Act passed in response to the 2008 financial crisis. It prohibits commercial banks from engaging in "proprietary trading" — trading securities, derivatives, and certain other financial instruments for their own account, using their own money, for profit. Since banks were major participants in derivative markets, this rule significantly reduced derivative activity. The rule exists because proprietary trading with depositor funds is seen as excessively risky and potentially destabilizing. Key exam point: Riskiest markets
          Your slides explicitly state that derivative markets are "also potentially the riskiest security." Derivatives allow massive leverage — small price movements in the underlying can cause enormous gains or losses. The 2008 crisis demonstrated this dramatically. AIG, an insurance company, nearly brought down the global financial system because of its exposure to credit default swaps (a type of derivative).

title: Flow of Funds: The Role of Financial Institutions
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Your slides illustrate two scenarios for how money flows through an economy: one without financial institutions (FIs) and one with them. Understanding this distinction explains why financial institutions exist and why they matter.

title: Without Financial Institutions (Direct Finance)
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: In a world without financial intermediaries, savers who have surplus funds would need to find borrowers directly. A farmer wanting to save money would need to directly lend to a corporation wanting to invest. This direct matching is extremely inefficient because of: information problems (how does the farmer evaluate the corporation's creditworthiness?), maturity mismatches (savers might want their money back in 6 months, corporations need it for 10 years), and risk concentration (the farmer bears all the risk of default).

title: With Financial Institutions (Indirect Finance)
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Financial institutions — banks, insurance companies, pension funds, mutual funds — solve all three problems by acting as intermediaries. A bank, for example, accepts short-term deposits from thousands of savers and makes long-term loans to corporations. It has expertise to evaluate creditworthiness, can pool risk across many borrowers, and transforms short-term savings into long-term investment. This financial intermediation dramatically increases the efficiency of capital allocation in the economy. Connection to fintech
          Fintech's "peer-to-peer" model attempts to recreate direct finance using technology to solve the information problem. Platforms like LendingClub match individual lenders with individual borrowers directly, bypassing traditional banks. This is why fintech is seen as a disruption to traditional FI business models — it makes direct finance feasible at scale.

title: Financial Technology (Fintech)
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Financial technology (fintech) refers to the use of technology to deliver financial solutions in a manner that competes with traditional financial methods. This is one of the most rapidly evolving areas of the financial world and is increasingly relevant to every aspect of banking, investing, and payments. The course specifically mentions cryptocurrencies (like Bitcoin) and blockchain as examples of fintech. Bitcoin is a decentralized digital currency that operates without a central bank or government. The underlying technology — blockchain — is a distributed ledger that records transactions in a way that is transparent, immutable, and requires no trusted central authority. Bitcoin's blockchain, for example, prevents double-spending (using the same digital currency twice) without needing a bank to verify transactions. Background: How does blockchain work (simply)?
          A blockchain is a chain of "blocks," each containing a batch of transactions. Every new block contains a cryptographic reference (called a hash) to the previous block, so altering any historical record would invalidate all subsequent blocks. This makes the ledger tamper-resistant. The blockchain is stored across thousands of computers simultaneously, so there's no single point of failure or control. Fintech risk is the risk that fintech firms could disrupt the business of traditional financial services firms through lost customers and lost revenue. This is sometimes called "disintermediation" — the process of cutting out the middleman (the bank). A student who sends money to a friend via Venmo is using fintech instead of a bank wire transfer; that's a fee the bank doesn't collect. Your slides also mention that fintech supports models of peer-to-peer mass collaboration — a reference to how technology enables direct connections between individuals who previously would have needed an institution to facilitate their interaction. Crowdfunding (Kickstarter), peer-to-peer lending (LendingClub), and robo-advisors (Betterment) are all examples of this model. Common misconception: Fintech vs. traditional finance
          Students sometimes assume fintech will completely replace traditional banks. In reality, the relationship is more complex: many banks are adopting fintech (PayPal, Apple Pay partnerships with banks), while true disruptors face regulatory hurdles since financial services are heavily regulated. The full impact of fintech on the financial system is still unfolding.

title: Globalization of Financial Markets and Institutions
subtitle: Chapter 1: Introduction to Financial Markets · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
U.S. financial markets remain the largest in the world, but the trend since the late 20th century has been clear: international markets are growing rapidly, and the boundaries between national financial systems are blurring. Capital flows across borders with increasing ease, and events in one market quickly affect markets on the other side of the world. Your text identifies seven key factors driving the globalization of financial markets: Growing foreign savings pools. As economies in Asia, the Middle East, and elsewhere developed, their citizens and institutions accumulated more savings — and those savings needed investment destinations beyond their home markets. International diversification seeking. Foreign investors increasingly turned to U.S. and other markets to diversify their portfolios and access investment opportunities unavailable at home. Better information access. The internet and financial information platforms (Bloomberg, Reuters, etc.) made it practical for investors to research and monitor foreign markets and investments. U.S. FIs offering global access. American financial institutions began offering retail and institutional clients low-cost access to foreign securities and emerging markets. The euro's impact. The introduction of the euro (1999) created a vast, unified currency zone with a single bond market that rivaled U.S. Treasuries in liquidity, fundamentally reshaping global capital flows. Pacific Basin and emerging market growth. Explosive economic growth in China, South Korea, India, and other Pacific Basin countries created enormous new stock markets and investment opportunities, drawing global capital. Deregulation in foreign countries. Many countries removed restrictions on cross-border capital flows, allowing international investors in and giving domestic investors access to global markets. The dark side of globalization: contagion
          Globalization has a drawback that becomes apparent in crises: financial problems in one country can rapidly spread worldwide. The 2008 U.S. mortgage crisis caused simultaneous market crashes in Europe, Asia, and emerging markets — because global investors held U.S. mortgage-backed securities. This "financial contagion" is a direct consequence of globalized capital markets. More interconnected markets mean faster, more widespread transmission of both growth and crises. Imagine you and a friend make a bet about tomorrow's weather. If it rains, you pay your friend $5. If it's sunny, your friend pays you $5. Notice what you've created: a financial agreement whose value depends entirely on something else (the weather). You didn't trade the weather itself — you traded a contract about the weather. That's basically a derivative. Now replace "weather" with "Apple's stock price" and "bet" with "option contract," and you have a real financial derivative. The option doesn't give you Apple stock; it gives you a contract whose value depends on what Apple's stock does. The underlying asset is what matters; the derivative is just the contract on top of it. Unlike a casual weather bet, financial derivatives are standardized, legally binding, often traded on exchanges, and can involve enormous sums. They also serve purposes beyond pure gambling — corporations use them to reduce risk (hedging), not just to speculate. And some derivatives (like mortgage-backed securities) are considerably more complex than a simple two-party agreement. A derivative security is a financial instrument whose payoff is linked to another, previously issued security or underlying asset (e.g., a stock, bond, currency, commodity, or index). Derivatives include futures (obligations to transact at a future date and price), options (rights but not obligations), swaps (exchanges of cash flows), and mortgage-backed securities (payoffs dependent on mortgage performance). They are traded in derivative security markets and generally involve bilateral agreements to exchange a standard quantity of an asset or cash flow at a predetermined price and at a specified future date. Imagine you're selling lemonade at a school fair, and the price of lemons is set in British pounds. You sell in dollars. You make a great profit in pounds — but when you convert your pounds to dollars at the end of the day, you discover the exchange rate changed and your dollar profit is way less than expected. Nothing went wrong with your lemonade business. The currency just moved against you. This is exactly what happens to U.S. companies earning money in foreign currencies. The business can be performing perfectly, but if the foreign currency weakens against the dollar, the dollar value of those earnings shrinks. That uncertainty is foreign exchange risk. Real foreign exchange risk is larger in scale and can be managed (hedged) using financial instruments. A lemonade stand can't easily hedge currency risk, but a multinational corporation can lock in a future exchange rate using currency futures or options. Also, exchange rates are driven by complex macroeconomic forces, not just supply and demand for one product. Foreign exchange risk is the sensitivity of the dollar value of cash flows on foreign investments to changes in the foreign currency's price relative to the dollar. U.S. investors receiving nondollar cash flows must convert them at the prevailing exchange rate, which can fluctuate due to interest rate differentials, inflation, political stability, and market forces. This risk can increase returns (if the foreign currency strengthens) or reduce them (if it weakens) independently of the underlying investment's performance. Suppose every person who wanted a loan had to personally find someone willing to lend to them. And the lender would need to research whether the borrower is trustworthy. And they'd need to agree on exactly how long the loan lasts. And the lender would have to be okay with tying up their money for years. This would be a nightmare — most loans would never happen. A bank solves all of this. It collects small deposits from millions of people (who want their money safe and accessible), and uses that pool to make large, long-term loans to businesses (who need big sums for a long time). The bank handles all the research, takes the risk, and pays depositors interest. It's a matchmaker that solves problems of size, timing, information, and risk all at once. Banks aren't perfect matchmakers — they can fail, misallocate capital, or take excessive risks (as in 2008). And they profit from the spread between what they pay depositors and what they charge borrowers, which creates incentives that don't always align with the economy's best interest. Financial institutions (FIs) act as intermediaries in the flow of funds from surplus-spending units (savers) to deficit-spending units (borrowers). They resolve market frictions including: information asymmetry (the FI has expertise to evaluate borrowers), maturity transformation (converting short-term deposits into long-term loans), and risk pooling (spreading individual default risks across a large portfolio). Without FIs, the direct finance model requires savers and borrowers to match their needs perfectly — an enormous inefficiency that would severely constrain capital allocation. Imagine you bought a concert ticket but can't go. If no one ever allowed ticket resale, you'd be stuck. You'd lose all your money, and you might never buy advance tickets again because of the risk. But if there's a resale market, you buy the ticket knowing you can sell it later if plans change. The original ticket sale (primary) still happens — but only because the resale market (secondary) exists. Stocks and bonds work the same way. Investors buy new shares from a company (primary market) because they know they can sell those shares later to other investors (secondary market) if they need cash. Take away the secondary market, and primary market fundraising collapses — nobody wants permanently illiquid assets. Concert tickets have simple, fixed value; financial securities have complex, continuously changing values based on company performance and economic conditions. Also, secondary markets serve the additional function of price discovery — they aggregate information from millions of investors to set prices that reflect collective wisdom about value. Ticket resale markets don't do this in the same sophisticated way. Secondary markets enable the trading of already-issued financial instruments and serve three critical functions: (1) liquidity — the ability to convert securities to cash quickly at fair market value, which lowers the risk premium investors demand on primary market purchases; (2) price discovery — continuous trading aggregates dispersed information into market prices that guide capital allocation; and (3) low transaction costs — liquid secondary markets enable efficient trading, reducing friction in the financial system. The existence of secondary markets is a prerequisite for functioning primary markets. The issuer (e.g., the corporation or government entity) receives the proceeds. The issuer is raising new capital by selling newly created securities. This distinguishes it from a secondary market transaction, where the selling investor receives the proceeds — not the original issuer. One year (12 months). Money market instruments have maturities of one year or less. Capital market instruments have maturities of more than one year (including equity/stocks, which technically have no maturity date at all). This maturity distinction also explains why capital market instruments have wider price fluctuations — more uncertainty over longer time horizons. The four main types are: (1) Futures — obligations to transact at a future date/price; (2) Options — rights but not obligations to buy/sell at a set price; (3) Swaps — agreements to exchange cash flows (e.g., fixed for floating interest rates); and (4) Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) — securities whose payoff is linked to mortgage loan payments. Liquidity is the ability to turn an asset into cash quickly at its fair market value. In secondary markets, liquidity means you can sell a security (stock, bond, etc.) rapidly without having to accept a price significantly below its true value. High liquidity is a major benefit of well-developed secondary markets and is one of the key reasons investors are willing to invest in primary markets in the first place. The Volcker Rule, implemented in 2014 as part of the Dodd-Frank Act. It restricts commercial banks from engaging in proprietary trading — using their own funds to trade securities, derivatives, and certain other instruments for profit. Since banks were major participants in derivative markets, this regulation significantly reduced derivative activity. Calculation:
            At ¥125/USD: ¥500M ÷ 125 = $4,000,000
            At ¥150/USD: ¥500M ÷ 150 = $3,333,333
            Loss due to currency movement: $666,667 This illustrates foreign exchange risk — the sensitivity of the dollar value of foreign cash flows to changes in exchange rates. The business performance was unchanged; the entire loss came from the yen weakening against the dollar. Companies can hedge this risk using currency futures or options in derivative markets. (a) This is a primary market transaction — NovaTech is issuing new securities and will receive the $50 million proceeds directly. (b) It is specifically called an Initial Public Offering (IPO) — the first time a company sells equity to the public. (c) After the IPO, investors will buy and sell NovaTech's existing shares in the secondary market (e.g., on a stock exchange like NASDAQ). NovaTech itself will not receive any money from these subsequent trades. (a) Money market — 90-day maturity is well under one year.
            (b) Capital market — 10-year maturity is far over one year.
            (c) Capital market — common stock has no maturity date; it's an infinite-horizon equity instrument and belongs in the capital market category.
            (d) Money market — 60-day maturity is under one year. Commercial paper is a classic money market instrument issued by corporations for short-term financing. Without FIs, the retiree would face major problems: (1) Information problem — they'd need to personally evaluate whether a borrower (say, a small business) is creditworthy; (2) Maturity mismatch — the retiree might need access to funds on short notice, but borrowers need long-term loans; (3) Risk concentration — lending all $200,000 to one borrower risks total loss if they default; (4) Search costs — finding a trustworthy borrower is time-consuming and expensive. A bank solves all of this: it pools the retiree's savings with many others, makes many diversified loans (spreading risk), handles credit evaluation, and offers demand-deposit accounts so the retiree can withdraw anytime. FIs exist because they solve these frictions far more efficiently than individuals can on their own. (a) This represents peer-to-peer mass collaboration enabled by fintech — technology is matching individual lenders with individual borrowers directly, without a financial intermediary. (b) The fintech risk to traditional FIs: fintech firms could disrupt FI business by attracting customers away and reducing revenue from lending, payments, or investment services. (c) Banks can respond by: adopting fintech technology themselves (digital lending platforms), partnering with fintech firms, reducing fees to compete on cost, leveraging advantages fintech lacks (regulatory trust, deposit insurance, established customer relationships, full-service financial products). (a) Primary and secondary markets: Mortgage lenders issued millions of mortgages (primary market). These were then securitized into MBS and sold to investors (primary market for MBS). Secondary markets for MBS provided liquidity, encouraging more mortgage origination. When MBS values collapsed, secondary market liquidity vanished — prices became impossible to determine and sellers couldn't find buyers. This seized up the entire financial system. (b) Derivative markets: MBS are derivatives whose value depends on mortgage repayment. When homeowners defaulted, MBS values collapsed. Credit default swaps (another derivative) had been sold in enormous quantities to insure against MBS defaults — when the payouts came due, institutions like AIG couldn't pay. The interconnected web of derivatives amplified and spread losses far beyond the original mortgages. (c) Globalization: Because of globalized financial markets, foreign banks and institutions had purchased U.S. MBS for their higher yields. When U.S. mortgages failed, losses spread instantly to banks in Europe, Asia, and elsewhere. This financial contagion — the rapid international transmission of financial distress — is a direct consequence of the globalized capital markets described in Chapter 1. (1) Who receives proceeds: In primary markets, the issuer (company or government) receives the capital raised. In secondary markets, the selling investor receives the proceeds — the issuer gets nothing from secondary trades. (2) Function: Primary markets serve capital formation — they channel new investment from savers to productive enterprises. Secondary markets serve liquidity, price discovery, and low-cost trading — they allow investors to manage their portfolios without being locked into positions. (3) Interdependence: If secondary markets ceased to exist, primary markets would essentially collapse. No rational investor would buy illiquid securities they could never sell — they'd demand enormous discounts or simply refuse. Without secondary markets, companies and governments couldn't raise capital at reasonable cost. Conversely, if primary markets ceased, secondary markets would wither too — there would be no new securities entering the system, and existing securities would gradually mature or be redeemed, leaving nothing to trade. FOR (derivatives are riskiest): Derivatives allow enormous leverage — a small price movement in the underlying can cause outsized gains or losses. They are complex, often opaque instruments that are difficult to value. Counterparty risk (the risk the other party defaults) is significant, especially in OTC markets. The 2008 crisis demonstrated how derivative interconnections can amplify losses system-wide. Speculative use of derivatives can dwarf the underlying market in size. AGAINST (or nuance): Derivatives, when used correctly, actually reduce risk by allowing hedging. Airlines use oil futures to lock in fuel costs; exporters use currency options to eliminate FX risk. Without derivatives, these risks would be borne by operating companies. The risk is in how derivatives are used, not in their existence. Also, all financial instruments carry risk; equity markets also involve speculation and leverage. Reasoned position: Derivatives are potentially riskiest when used speculatively with high leverage, or when complex structured products obscure underlying risk. But labeling them categorically "riskiest" ignores their risk-reduction role. The danger lies in misuse, opacity, and the systemic interconnections they create — not in derivatives as a class of instruments. Potential benefits (drawing on the 7 globalization factors): Deregulation can attract foreign capital pools (Factor 1) seeking diversification (Factor 2). It expands the investor base (Factor 7), potentially lowering the cost of capital for domestic firms. Access to foreign financial institutions (Factor 4) can improve financial services quality. Integration into global capital markets increases liquidity and information transparency. Risks: Greater openness exposes the country to financial contagion — when global markets crash, foreign investors may rapidly withdraw capital ("sudden stops"), destabilizing the economy. Financial globalization can increase inequality if only wealthy or institutional investors benefit. Domestic financial institutions may lack the sophistication to compete or to manage new instruments and risks. Rapid capital inflows can create asset bubbles; rapid outflows can cause currency crises. Advice: Deregulate gradually, with strong domestic regulatory frameworks in place first. Build robust banking supervision, capital requirements, and crisis management mechanisms before fully opening capital accounts. Learn from the experiences of countries like South Korea (1997 currency crisis after rapid liberalization) and Chile (more gradual, successful liberalization). Threat perspective: Fintech directly attacks FI revenue streams. P2P lending bypasses bank interest margins. Digital wallets and payment apps replace bank transaction fees. Robo-advisors undercut wealth management fees. Cryptocurrency potentially displaces bank deposits and central bank monetary policy. If fintech scales, traditional FIs face structural revenue decline and loss of their intermediary role. Opportunity perspective: Fintech can make financial markets more efficient, inclusive, and accessible. FIs can adopt fintech to cut costs, improve services, and reach underserved markets. Banks have regulatory advantages (deposit insurance, lender of last resort), established trust, and full-service capabilities that fintech startups lack. Many fintech companies ultimately seek banking licenses or partnerships, integrating into the traditional system rather than replacing it. Conclusion: Fintech is simultaneously a threat and an opportunity. It's a threat to FIs that fail to innovate and cling to legacy business models. It's an opportunity for adaptive FIs and for the financial system as a whole — fintech drives efficiency, reduces barriers to access, and forces competitive improvements. The most likely outcome is a hybrid financial system where traditional FIs and fintech coexist and increasingly converge, rather than one replacing the other. title: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates
subtitle: Financial Markets & Institutions
content: From loanable funds theory to the Fisher effect, yield curves, and time value of money — with worked examples and exam-ready exercises.

title: Why Interest Rates Matter
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Interest rates are among the most powerful forces in finance. They are the price of money — what you pay to borrow it and what you earn by lending it. Nominal interest rates are the rates actually observed in financial markets: the 5% on your savings account, the 7% on a mortgage, the 4% yield on a Treasury bond. These rates directly affect the price of virtually every financial security, and their movements ripple through every corner of the economy. When interest rates rise, existing bond prices fall (because new bonds offer higher yields, making old ones less valuable). When rates fall, borrowing becomes cheaper, stimulating business investment and consumer spending. Central banks like the Federal Reserve spend enormous energy trying to understand, forecast, and influence interest rates — which is why understanding where interest rates come from is foundational to all of finance. Background: Nominal vs. Real interest rates
          A "nominal" rate is the rate you actually see quoted. A "real" rate adjusts for inflation. If your savings account pays 5% but inflation is 3%, your real return is approximately 2% — you're only gaining 2% in actual purchasing power. This distinction is critical throughout Chapter 2 and reappears constantly in finance.

title: The Loanable Funds Theory
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The loanable funds theory is the foundational framework for understanding interest rate determination. It views equilibrium interest rates as the result of the supply of and demand for loanable funds in financial markets — just like the price of any good is determined by supply and demand. When supply of funds increases or demand decreases, interest rates fall. When demand increases or supply decreases, interest rates rise.

title: Suppliers of Loanable Funds
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The "supply of loanable funds" describes capital provided to financial markets by those with surplus money. Supply increases as interest rates rise — higher rates incentivize saving and lending. The major suppliers are:

title: What Shifts the Supply Curve
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
At any given interest rate, the supply of loanable funds shifts when these factors change: As overall wealth increases, people and institutions supply more funds. A growing economy generates more surplus capital looking for investment. Wealth ↑ → Supply ↑ If financial securities become riskier (more volatility, more defaults), suppliers pull back. Risk ↑ → Supply ↓ (risk-averse investors demand higher rates before they'll supply funds) If households anticipate upcoming expenses (holiday season, medical costs), they save more beforehand. Spending needs ↑ → Supply ↑ (temporarily, as they accumulate funds) When the Federal Reserve allows economic expansion (e.g., low rates, bond purchases), it injects money into the system. Expansionary policy → Supply ↑. Contractionary policy → Supply ↓ Shift Factor 5: Foreign economic conditions
          When domestic economic conditions improve, domestic suppliers have more to invest, increasing the local supply of funds. When foreign economies improve, foreign investors may redirect funds home rather than to U.S. markets — reducing the foreign supply of funds to U.S. markets. These are asymmetric effects worth remembering.

title: Demanders of Loanable Funds
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The "demand for loanable funds" describes the total net demand for borrowing by fund users. Demand generally increases as interest rates fall — cheaper borrowing costs make more projects viable. The major borrowers are:

title: What Shifts the Demand Curve
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Three key factors shift demand at any given rate: 1. Utility/return from borrowed funds. If the expected return from an investment (funded by borrowed money) rises, businesses are willing to borrow more even at the same rate. Expected project return ↑ → Demand ↑. 2. Non-price conditions on borrowing. Even if the interest rate stays the same, if banks require more collateral, impose restrictive covenants (limits on what borrowers can do with funds), or charge higher fees, effective borrowing becomes more expensive. More restrictive non-price conditions → Demand ↓. 3. Economic growth vs. stagnation. During economic expansion, businesses borrow more to invest in new capacity and inventory. During recessions, borrowing plummets because investment opportunities look poor. Growth → Demand ↑; Stagnation → Demand ↓. Common misconception: Government as supplier vs. demander
          Students often forget that governments appear on BOTH sides of the loanable funds market. The government can supply funds (when running a surplus or managing reserves) AND demand funds (when running a deficit, which is most of the time in the U.S.). In practice, the U.S. government is a massive net borrower, so it primarily shifts the demand curve rightward, putting upward pressure on interest rates — a phenomenon called "crowding out."

title: Determinants of Interest Rates for Individual Securities
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The loanable funds theory explains market-level interest rates. But why do different securities trade at different rates? A 10-year Treasury bond, a corporate junk bond, and a municipal bond all have different yields — even in the same market, at the same time. The answer lies in the components that make up any security's interest rate: Each component compensates the investor for a specific type of cost or risk. Let's unpack each one.

title: 1. Inflation Premium (IP) and the Fisher Effect
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: Inflation is the continual increase in the price level of a basket of goods and services. It is measured in the U.S. using the Consumer Price Index (CPI) or Producer Price Index (PPI). The key insight: if you lend money at 5% for a year, but prices rise 4%, your purchasing power only grew by 1%. Investors demand compensation for expected inflation — the inflation premium (IP). The relationship between the real risk-free rate, expected inflation, and the nominal interest rate is captured by the Fisher effect, named after economist Irving Fisher: The real risk-free rate (RFR) is the interest rate that would exist on a risk-free security if no inflation were expected. It represents society's "time preference for money" — how much people prefer consumption today over future consumption. Higher preference for spending now → higher RFR. The RFR is theoretical; in practice, the U.S. Treasury bill rate (adjusted for inflation) is used as a proxy.

title: 2. Default Risk Premium (DRP)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Default risk is the risk that a security issuer will fail to make promised interest and principal payments. The higher the default risk, the higher the interest rate investors demand. The compensation for bearing this risk is the default risk premium (DRP): Background: Credit rating agencies
          Companies like Moody's, Standard & Poor's (S&P), and Fitch assign credit ratings to bond issuers. AAA/Aaa rated bonds are considered investment-grade with very low default risk; BB/Ba and below are "junk" or high-yield bonds with higher default risk — and correspondingly higher DRPs. The spread between AAA corporate bonds and Treasury yields fluctuates with economic conditions, widening sharply during crises.

title: 3. Liquidity Risk Premium (LRP)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Liquidity risk is the risk that a security cannot be sold quickly at a predictable price with low transaction costs. Highly liquid assets (like U.S. Treasury securities) trade constantly with tiny bid-ask spreads. Illiquid assets (like thinly traded municipal bonds) may require selling at a significant discount if you need cash quickly. Investors who accept illiquid securities demand a liquidity risk premium (LRP). Think of it this way: a house and a U.S. Treasury bond might offer the same annual "return," but you'd demand extra compensation for the house because selling it takes months. That extra compensation is the liquidity premium. Two faces of liquidity risk premium
          LRP can arise in two ways: (1) actual illiquidity — the security is hard to trade; and (2) maturity-related sensitivity — even liquid long-term bonds are riskier than short-term bonds because their prices are more sensitive to interest rate changes. Your notes specifically mention this second source. A 30-year bond's price drops far more than a 1-year bond when rates rise by the same amount — this duration risk is sometimes compensated with an LRP.

title: 4. Special Covenant/Feature Premium (SCP)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Certain contractual features written into a security's terms affect its interest rate. These provisions can either benefit or burden the investor: Key rule to remember
          Features that benefit the investor → lower interest rate (investor accepts less yield for the added value). Features that benefit the issuer → higher interest rate (investor demands more yield to compensate for the disadvantage). Callability is the classic "issuer benefit" that students often forget raises the yield.

title: Term Structure of Interest Rates and the Yield Curve
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: The term structure of interest rates is a comparison of yields on securities that are identical in every way except their maturity. Plotted on a chart, this relationship is called the yield curve. The yield curve is one of the most important and closely watched tools in finance because its shape signals expectations about the economy and future interest rates. The change in required interest rates as maturity changes is called the maturity premium (MP). The MP can be positive, negative, or zero — which gives rise to three common yield curve shapes:

title: Three Theories Explaining Yield Curve Shape
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Long-term rates equal the average of expected future short-term rates. If the market expects short-term rates to rise, long-term rates will be higher today — creating an upward slope. If rates are expected to fall, the curve inverts. This theory implies the yield curve reflects pure expectations, nothing more. Extends expectations theory by adding a liquidity (risk) premium for longer maturities. Investors are risk-averse and dislike locking money up for long periods. So long-term rates = expected future short-term rates + a liquidity premium. This means yield curves will be upward biased even if rates are expected to stay flat. Different investors prefer different maturities (their "habitats") and won't easily move between maturity segments. Banks prefer short-term; pension funds prefer long-term. Supply and demand within each segment independently determines yields. The curve shape is driven by segment-specific supply/demand, not expectations. Why does an inverted yield curve matter so much?
          An inverted yield curve (short-term rates higher than long-term rates) is historically one of the most reliable predictors of a recession. It signals that the market expects the central bank to cut rates in the future (because the economy will weaken), making long-term rates lower than short-term rates today. Every U.S. recession since 1955 was preceded by an inverted yield curve. Expectations Theory Calculation: Example
          Suppose the 1-year rate today is 3%, the expected 1-year rate next year is 4%, and the expected 1-year rate in two years is 5%. Under the Unbiased Expectations Theory, the 3-year rate today should be approximately (3% + 4% + 5%) / 3 = 4%. The yield curve is upward-sloping because rates are expected to rise.

title: Time Value of Money (TVM)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
The time value of money is the foundational concept underlying all of finance: a dollar received today is worth more than a dollar received in the future. Why? Because a dollar today can be invested to earn a return, making it worth more than a dollar received later. This creates a framework for comparing cash flows that occur at different points in time. The 3 Rules of Time Travel (from your TVM supplement)
          Rule 1: You can only compare/combine cash flows at the same point in time. Rule 2: To move a cash flow forward, you must compound it (multiply by (1+r)). Rule 3: To move a cash flow backward, you must discount it (divide by (1+r)). Everything in TVM follows from these three rules.

subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: FV Example: Invest $1,000 today at 10% for 2 years: PV Example: Need $10,000 in 1 year. Can earn 7%. How much to invest today?

title: Annuity Formulas
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: An annuity is a series of equal cash flows received at fixed intervals over a finite number of periods. Your mortgage payment, car payment, or annual bond coupon are all annuities. A bond pays $50 per year for 3 years, then $1,000 at maturity. Discount rate = 6%. Find PV of the coupon payments.

title: Perpetuity Formulas
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content: A perpetuity is an annuity that pays forever. It sounds complex, but the formula is beautifully simple:

title: Solving for Rate (r) and Time (n)
subtitle: Chapter 2: Determinants of Interest Rates · Financial Markets & Institutions
content:
Critical: Simple vs. Compound Interest
          Simple interest earns interest only on the principal. Compound interest earns interest on principal PLUS previously earned interest. At $100 × 5% for 2 years: Simple FV = $110. Compound FV = $110.25. The difference grows enormously over long periods. At 200 years: Simple = $1,200. Compound = $4,471,898. Every finance problem assumes compound interest unless explicitly told otherwise. Calculator sign convention (critical for avoiding errors)
          On the BA-II Plus, cash outflows are negative, inflows are positive. If you invest $1,000 today (outflow), enter PV = −1,000. The calculator will return FV as a positive number (you receive it). Always clear TVM registers (2ND → FV) between problems. Forgetting the sign convention is the #1 cause of calculator errors on exams. Imagine a snowball rolling downhill. The bigger it gets, the more snow it picks up — so it keeps accelerating. Compound interest works the same way. Each year, you earn interest not just on your original money, but also on all the interest you've already earned. So the pile grows, which means next year's interest is even bigger, which makes the pile even bigger, and so on forever. Put $100 at 10%. After year 1: $110. After year 2: not $120 (simple) but $121 — because you earned 10% on $110, not just $100. That extra $1 seems trivial. But over 30 years, your $100 becomes $1,745 with compounding, versus $400 with simple interest. The snowball effect is enormous. A snowball eventually gets too big to roll and stops. Money, in theory, can compound forever without stopping (though inflation, taxes, and market returns vary in practice). Also, compounding requires reinvesting returns — if you spend your interest each year, it's essentially simple interest. Compound interest applies the interest rate to the growing accumulated balance, not just the original principal. The future value formula is FV = PV × (1+r)ⁿ. The exponent n is what creates the exponential (not linear) growth. Simple interest grows as FV = PV × (1 + r×n), which is linear. The mathematical difference between linear and exponential growth is what makes compounding so powerful over long horizons. Imagine you lend your friend $100 so they can buy you pizza when they repay you next year. But you know pizza will cost 10% more next year due to inflation. If your friend just gives back $100, you can buy less pizza than you could today. So you'd tell your friend: "Pay me $110 — $100 to replace what I lent you, and $10 to cover the fact that pizza costs more now." That extra $10 is the inflation premium. Lenders in financial markets do exactly this: they add expected inflation to their required return so they don't lose purchasing power. If I want a 2% real return and expect 3% inflation, I'll demand 5% nominal. The real world is more complex because the actual inflation that occurs may differ from expected inflation. Lenders are compensated for expected inflation at the time of the loan; if actual inflation turns out higher, they lose purchasing power anyway. Also, the Fisher effect is empirically approximate — the precise relationship involves a multiplicative cross-product term that matters at high inflation rates. The Fisher effect states: (1+i) = (1+RFR) × (1+E(IP)), where i is the nominal rate, RFR is the real risk-free rate, and E(IP) is expected inflation. The approximation i ≈ RFR + E(IP) ignores the cross-product term RFR×E(IP), which is small at low rates but significant at high rates. The Fisher effect implies that nominal rates should rise one-for-one with changes in expected inflation, holding the real rate constant — an empirically tested prediction with strong (but imperfect) support in the data. Imagine there's a rumor that next summer will be extremely hot. Water vendors right now charge $1 per bottle (that's the current "short-term price"). But vendors willing to lock in a price for next summer are charging $3 per bottle because of the expected shortage. That "forward price" is higher because people expect conditions to change. The yield curve works similarly. If people expect interest rates to rise in the future (because the economy will be booming), they demand higher yields today on long-term loans. Why? Because if rates rise in two years, a long-term bond paying today's rate will look stingy. The curve slopes upward. If people expect a recession (rates to fall), the curve may invert — short-term rates are high now but expected to fall. The yield curve reflects multiple forces simultaneously, not just expectations: risk premiums for holding long-term bonds, segmented market supply/demand, Federal Reserve policy interventions, and global capital flows. No single theory fully explains all yield curve shapes at all times — that's why three competing theories exist. The term structure of interest rates plots yields against maturities for securities with identical characteristics except maturity. The maturity premium (MP) can be positive, negative, or zero. Three theories explain its shape: (1) Unbiased Expectations — long rates equal the geometric average of expected future short rates; (2) Liquidity Premium — long rates include a risk premium above expectations; (3) Market Segmentation — supply and demand in each maturity "habitat" independently determines yields, with no arbitrage across segments. Suppose someone offers you a choice: $1,000 right now, or a guaranteed $1,000 exactly one year from now. Any rational person takes the money now — because you can invest it and have more than $1,000 in a year. The future $1,000 is worth less to you today because you're giving up the ability to earn a return on it. To compare them fairly, we need to ask: "How much would I need to invest today to have $1,000 in one year?" If you can earn 10%, the answer is $1,000 ÷ 1.10 = $909.09. So the present value of $1,000 received in one year at 10% is $909.09. We "discounted" the future value back to today's terms. This assumes certainty — guaranteed future payments. In reality, future cash flows are usually uncertain. The discount rate used in practice incorporates not just the risk-free rate but also risk premiums to account for that uncertainty. Riskier cash flows get discounted at higher rates, making their present value lower — reflecting that investors demand compensation for uncertainty. Present value (PV) is the current value of a future cash flow, discounted at an appropriate rate. For a lump sum: PV = FV/(1+r)ⁿ. For a stream of cash flows: PV = Σ Cₙ/(1+r)ⁿ. The discount rate r represents the opportunity cost of capital — the return available on the best alternative investment of comparable risk. Higher r → lower PV. Longer horizon → lower PV. This framework underlies all of security valuation, capital budgeting, and financial decision-making. Equilibrium interest rates are determined by the supply of and demand for loanable funds in financial markets — exactly like price determination in a product market. When supply of funds exceeds demand, rates fall; when demand exceeds supply, rates rise. The theory categorizes all market participants (consumers, businesses, governments, foreign participants) as either net suppliers or demanders of funds. ij* = f(IP, RFR, DRPj, LRPj, SCPj, MPj) (1) IP — Inflation Premium: compensates for expected loss in purchasing power
            (2) RFR — Real Risk-Free Rate: base return for time preference with no risk/inflation
            (3) DRP — Default Risk Premium: compensates for risk of issuer failing to pay
            (4) LRP — Liquidity Risk Premium: compensates for difficulty selling at fair price quickly
            (5) SCP — Special Covenant Premium: adjusts for features like callability (↑ rate) or tax-exemption (↓ rate)
            (6) MP — Maturity Premium: accounts for sensitivity to rate changes with longer term Compound FV = PV × (1+r)ⁿ = 1,000 × (1.08)³ = 1,000 × 1.2597 = $1,259.71 Simple FV = PV × (1 + r×n) = 1,000 × (1 + 0.08×3) = 1,000 × 1.24 = $1,240.00 Difference: $19.71. This gap grows significantly over longer horizons — compound interest's "snowball effect" becomes increasingly powerful over time. At 6%: 72 / 6 = 12 years
            At 12%: 72 / 12 = 6 years Doubling the interest rate cuts the doubling time in half. Check: $1,000 at 6% for 12 years = 1,000 × (1.06)¹² = $2,012 ✓ (approximately doubles). The Rule of 72 is a quick mental math shortcut — handy on exams when you need a rough estimate without a calculator. PV = C / r = 500 / 0.05 = $10,000 This is elegantly simple: a stream of $500 per year forever, discounted at 5%, is worth $10,000 today. Makes intuitive sense — $10,000 invested at 5% generates $500/year in interest, so $10,000 is the fair price for that cash flow stream. Approximate: i ≈ RFR + E(IP) = 1.5% + 2.8% = 4.3% Exact (Fisher): (1+i) = (1.015)(1.028) = 1.04342, so i = 4.342% The cross-product (0.015 × 0.028 = 0.000042 = 0.042%) is very small at these rate levels, so the approximation works well. At 20% inflation and 5% real rate, the difference would be 1% — much more significant. Formula: PV = FV / (1+r)ⁿ = 150,000 / (1.08)¹⁷ (1.08)¹⁷ = 3.7000 (approx) PV = 150,000 / 3.7000 = $40,540.34 Calculator: N=17, I/Y=8, FV=150,000, PMT=0 → CPT PV = -40,540.34 Investing ~$40,500 today at 8% grows to $150,000 in 17 years. This is the power of early saving and compound growth. Formula: PV = C × [1 − 1/(1+r)ⁿ] / r = 350 × [1 − 1/(1.005)⁴⁸] / 0.005 (1.005)⁴⁸ = 1.2705; 1/1.2705 = 0.7871; 1 − 0.7871 = 0.2129 = 350 × 0.2129 / 0.005 = 350 × 42.58 = $14,904.22 Calculator: N=48, I/Y=0.5, PMT=350, FV=0 → CPT PV = -14,904.22 Note: When payments are monthly, the period interest rate must be monthly (6%/12 = 0.5%) and N is the number of months, not years. DRP = Corporate yield − Treasury yield = 4.5% − 3.8% = 0.70% (70 basis points) The DRP compensates investors for the (small, since AAA) risk that the corporation might default, compared to the effectively zero-default risk of the U.S. Treasury. Callability: The yield would be HIGHER. A callable bond gives the issuer the right to repay early — typically when rates fall. This hurts the investor (who loses the above-market coupon just when they'd most want it). Investors demand a higher yield (call premium) to compensate for this disadvantage. Callable bonds always have higher yields than equivalent non-callable bonds. Exact calculation: (1 + 3yr rate)³ = (1.04)(1.05)(1.06) = 1.15668 3yr rate = (1.15668)^(1/3) − 1 = 1.04993 − 1 = 4.993% ≈ 5% Approximation: (4% + 5% + 6%) / 3 = 5% The 3-year yield today (≈5%) is higher than the current 1-year rate (4%) because the market expects rates to rise. This creates an upward-sloping yield curve at these maturities. (a) Supply of funds: Higher interest rates incentivize more saving (higher return for deferring consumption). Supply curve shifts rightward — more funds available at each rate level. Foreign investors may also move funds into U.S. markets for higher yields, increasing supply further. (b) Demand for funds: Higher rates make borrowing more expensive. Businesses postpone investments that no longer meet return thresholds (projects must earn more than the higher borrowing cost). Household demand for mortgages and consumer credit falls. Demand curve shifts leftward — fewer funds demanded at each rate level. (c) Fed's strategy: By tightening monetary policy, the Fed is deliberately reducing the supply of reserves and raising rates, which: (1) reduces spending/investment (cools the economy); (2) reduces the demand for funds; and (3) works through the Fisher effect — by bringing actual inflation down, it reduces the inflation premium component of all interest rates, eventually allowing nominal rates to fall. The mechanism is intentional monetary contraction to reduce aggregate demand and cool price pressures. Unbiased Expectations Theory: Long-term rates = average of expected future short-term rates. Predicts flat curves when rates are expected to stay flat, inverted when expected to fall. Assumes risk-neutral investors who are indifferent between short and long maturities. Liquidity Premium Theory: Extends expectations by adding a positive risk premium for longer maturities. Even if rates are expected to stay flat, the curve slopes upward because investors demand extra return for holding longer-term, less liquid securities. Explains why curves are usually upward-sloping. Market Segmentation Theory: Investors and institutions prefer specific maturities based on their liability structures. Banks prefer short-term (matching short-term deposits); pension funds prefer long-term (matching long-term obligations). Supply and demand within each segment determines yields independently. The shape reflects segment-specific imbalances, not expectations. Best explanation for persistent upward slope: Liquidity Premium Theory. It preserves the expectations logic while adding a realistic behavioral element: investors are risk-averse and demand compensation for holding longer, more volatile securities. This naturally biases the curve upward even when rates are expected to be flat — which matches empirical data far better than the pure expectations theory. Why munis yield less: Municipal bond interest is exempt from federal income tax (and often state/local taxes in the issuing state). This is a "special feature" (SCP) that benefits the investor. Under the SCP framework, features that benefit investors → lower interest rate. Investors accept a lower nominal yield on munis because their after-tax return can equal or exceed a higher-yielding taxable bond's after-tax return. Calculation illustration: An investor in the 35% tax bracket compares a 4% muni to a 6% corporate bond. After-tax corporate return = 6% × (1−0.35) = 3.9%. After-tax muni return = 4% (no tax). The muni wins despite the lower stated yield. The tax-equivalent yield formula: Tax-equiv yield = Muni yield / (1 − tax rate) = 4% / 0.65 = 6.15%. So the muni is equivalent to a 6.15% taxable bond. Best investor for munis: High-income investors in high tax brackets — they gain the most from the tax exemption. Low-income investors (low tax bracket) benefit less from tax exemption and may find taxable bonds better. Tax-exempt institutional investors (pension funds, IRAs) gain nothing from the exemption and should avoid munis entirely. (a) PV = C/(r−g) = 100/(0.08−0.03) = 100/0.05 = $2,000 (b) If g rises to 5%: PV = 100/(0.08−0.05) = 100/0.03 = $3,333Higher growth rate → smaller denominator → much higher PV. A 2% increase in growth rate increased value by 67%! (c) If r falls to 5% (g stays at 3%): PV = 100/(0.05−0.03) = 100/0.02 = $5,000The denominator (r−g) narrows from 0.05 to 0.02, more than doubling the value. This shows why falling interest rates cause massive increases in asset prices — they reduce the discount rate that shrinks all future cash flows. Warning: If r ≤ g, the formula breaks down (you get zero or negative denominator). This is theoretically impossible for a real asset — in practice, growth rates must be below the discount rate in the long run. Your friend has a point, but it's more nuanced: Why they may be right (current risk): When rates are high, long-term bond prices are low (inverse relationship). If you buy now and rates rise further, bond prices fall more — and long-term bonds fall far more than short-term bonds for the same rate increase (this is the maturity premium / duration effect). If you need to sell before maturity, you could face capital losses. Why they may be wrong (opportunity): High rates today mean high yields — you're locking in a high return for many years. If rates fall (which often happens after rate hikes), your bond's price rises and you can sell at a capital gain. Historically, after the Fed tightens aggressively, rates eventually fall, creating substantial capital gains on long-term bonds. Additional risks to consider: DRP — is the bond's issuer creditworthy? During rate-hike cycles, economic stress can increase default risk on corporate bonds. LRP — can you sell the bond quickly if needed? Long-term corporate bonds can be illiquid in stressed markets. Reinvestment risk — if rates fall, future coupon reinvestment earns less. Conclusion: Whether it's a "bad time" depends on your investment horizon and whether you hold to maturity. If held to maturity, interest rate risk is irrelevant (you get par value + coupons regardless). If you might need to sell early, high-rate environments are indeed risky for long-term bonds. IP Expected inflation — measured by CPI/PPI changes RFR Time preference for money with no risk. Proxy: inflation-adjusted T-bill rate DRP Yield − equivalent Treasury yield. Higher for lower-rated issuers LRP Premium for illiquid securities or maturity-related price sensitivity SCP Tax-exempt → rate ↓. Callable → rate ↑. Convertible → rate ↓ MP Can be +, −, or 0. Creates yield curve shape Pitfall 1 Monthly rates: use r/12 and n×12 for monthly cash flows Pitfall 2 Sign convention on calculator: outflows negative, inflows positive Pitfall 3 g must be less than r in growing perpetuity formula Pitfall 4 Callable bonds → higher yield (not lower) — issuer benefits Pitfall 5 Simple vs compound: finance always uses compound unless stated Pitfall 6 Fisher: exact formula (multiplicative), not just additive Normal ↑ Long-term rates > short-term. Rates expected to rise. Most common shape. Inverted ↓ Short-term > long-term. Rates expected to fall. Predicts recession. Flat → All maturities similar yield. Transition between normal and inverted.
